title: Max Pooling: Keeping the Strongest Signal
concept: Max pooling looks at each local window and keeps only the largest value, preserving the strongest activation while shrinking the feature map.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What key thing does pooling NOT have that convolution does?
exercises.explain: Right. Pooling has no learned weights, so its behavior is set by a simple rule — like taking the maximum.

cards.title: Only the loudest voice gets through
cards.body: Imagine four people shouting into one microphone at once. If the microphone only records the loudest voice, the smaller voices disappear. Max pooling does that with activations: in each tiny region, the strongest signal wins.

cards.title: What is max pooling?
cards.body: Max pooling looks at a small window, such as 2x2, and outputs the largest value in that window. It keeps the strongest activation from each local region while shrinking the overall map.

cards.title: Winner takes the tile
cards.body: Think of each pooling window as a tiny contest. Four numbers compete, but only one gets to move on. The winner is always the biggest number in that tile.

cards.title: Finding the winner
cards.body: Suppose one 2x2 window contains [1, 5; 2, 4]. Max pooling compares the four values and keeps only 5. The other three values are dropped for that window.

cards.title: Max pooling does not add numbers up
cards.body: Because it looks at a group of values, some people think max pooling somehow combines all of them. It does not. It keeps exactly one value: the largest one. No summing, no averaging, no learned weighting.

cards.title: Inside One Max-Pooling Window
cards.body: What happens to one local region:

exercises.prompt: If a max-pooling window contains the values [2, 7; 1, 6], what comes out?
exercises.explain: Correct — max pooling keeps the single largest value, which is 7.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Max pooling keeps every value in the window, just in sorted order.
exercises.explain: False. Max pooling keeps only one value from the window: the largest one.

exercises.sentence: In max pooling, the winner takes the ___.
exercises.explain: Tile fits the idea: one local window, one winner.

exercises.prompt: Which description best matches max pooling in a feature map?

options.text: Each small region keeps its strongest activation and drops the weaker nearby ones
Each small region learns a fresh set of weights before deciding what to output
Each small region gets expanded into more numbers than it started with

exercises.explain: Exactly — max pooling is a fixed-rule way to preserve the strongest local response.

exercises.prompt: A 4x4 feature map is split into four non-overlapping 2x2 windows: top-left [1, 3; 5, 2], top-right [0, 4; 7, 1], bottom-left [6, 2; 3, 1], and bottom-right [8, 5; 2, 4]. What is the 2x2 output after max pooling?
exercises.explain: Exactly. Each window chooses its strongest value: 5, 7, 6, and 8. That is max pooling in action. Next: what if we want the region summary to reflect all the values, not just the winner?
